nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. In line 3, what does the simile “like clouds across the sky” help the reader understand about the horses in Euphemia’s sketches? Choices: A: They are drawn sloppily. B: They look like they are in motion. C: They are getting tangled up with each other. D: They look like they are trotting through fog.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. How do lines 14 through 16 contribute to the development of the plot? Choices: A: They establish Marisa’s problem. B: They emphasize Marisa’s hopefulness. C: They contrast Marisa’s artistic abilities with Euphemia’s. D: They illustrate Marisa’s determination to not let Luke bother her.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. Which phrase best conveys the tone in lines 1 through 30? Choices: A: “They’re always running wild and free. . .” (lines 2 and 3) B: “He also calls me Messy.” (lines 8 and 9) C: “Next I roughed in most of the body. . .” (lines 17 and 18) D: “I scratched a big X through my earless, macaroni-legged horse, . . .” (line 26)

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. Read this sentence from line 32. I brooded about it all through dinner. What effect does the word “brooded” have in the story? Choices: A: It shows Marisa’s anxiety about her abilities. B: It reveals Marisa’s motives for drawing. C: It emphasizes how Marisa is growing as a character. D: It indicates Marisa has a major decision to make.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. How do lines 36 through 38 help convey the theme of the story? Choices: A: They show that some situations take time to change. B: They prove that practice can help natural talents to develop. C: They suggest that inspiration may come in unexpected ways. D: They demonstrate that new ideas will eventually be accepted.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Which sentence best explains why Marisa’s final horse drawing was different than her first tries? Choices: A: “Everything looked special in that light, even the scraggly horses next door.” (lines 37 and 38) B: “I noticed the curves of their muscles, the shadows on their faces, the shine along their backs.” (lines 42 through 44) C: “An idea began to take shape in my mind, and just then the cinnamon horse turned its head toward me and nodded three times.” (lines 49 and 50) D: “I choose a deep brown, pulling it across my paper in the shape of the chocolate horse.” (line 52)

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. How does Marisa change while watching her neighbors’ horses? Choices: A: She realizes that Euphemia’s horses do not look realistic, so she decides to try to draw better pictures than her friend. B: She decides to try a different way of drawing and is proud of her work. C: She realizes she can never be an artist like Euphemia but wants to draw like her anyway. D: She finally learns that drawing horses is easier with proper lighting and art supplies.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. Read line 15 of the story. “You call this one only fair?” he asked. “It is a gem for the king.” What does this line suggest about the father? Choices: A: He has not looked at the pearl as closely as Ramón has. B: He does not think that Ramón is correct about the pearl. C: He is testing Ramón’s confidence in judging the pearl’s value. D: He is teaching Ramón about the pearl’s quality.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. Which detail from the story best supports the idea that Ramón is becoming an expert at judging pearls? Choices: A: “‘For this one,’ he said, ‘what is your notation?’” (lines 11 and 12) B: “‘For a poor king,’ I said.” (line 16) C: “‘With a little care the flaw can be peeled away,’ he said.” (lines 19 and 20) D: “I would like to go today.” (line 29)

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. Why is the father reluctant to bring Ramón on a pearl‑diving trip? Choices: A: He is concerned for Ramón’s safety. B: He needs Ramón to evaluate more pearls. C: He thinks Ramón is still too young to sail. D: He is unsure Ramón is ready to dive.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. In line 55, why does Ramón suggest that he will “stay on deck while the others dive”? Choices: A: His father needs him to help with other jobs on the boat. B: He realizes that his father will never actually let him go. C: His father has convinced him that it is too dangerous. D: He is trying to gradually change his father’s mind.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q19.alt: Question 19. How does line 57 best contribute to the development of the story? Choices: A: by signaling a turning point B: by providing a solution to the problem C: by comparing the characters’ actions D: by introducing a new conflict

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q20.alt: Question 20. How does the father change during the story? Choices: A: He becomes concerned about a diver’s health. B: He begins to acknowledge Ramón’s maturity. C: He becomes frustrated by Ramón’s persistence. D: He stops worrying about his family business.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q21.alt: Question 21. The author develops Ramón’s point of view in the story mostly by Choices: A: describing Ramón’s fear of pearl diving B: including Ramón’s analysis of the pearl C: describing how Ramón feels about his father D: including dialogue between Ramón and his father

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q29.alt: Question 29. In lines 4 through 12, the author explains why scientists are trying to find a way to produce spider silk using goats by showing Choices: A: possible uses for spider silk B: the popularity of spider silk C: how easy spider silk is to use D: how quickly spider silk can be developed

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q30.alt: Question 30. Which statement best explains an advantage of using goats rather than spiders for the production of silk? Choices: A: Goats produce stronger silk than spiders do. B: Scientists can insert genes into goats but not into spiders. C: Spider proteins in goat milk can be spun into silk. D: Goats are bigger than spiders and are much easier to raise.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q31.alt: Question 31. What did Jeffrey Turner discover about using Nubian goats for possible silk production? Choices: A: Nubian goats already make a similar substance. B: Nubian goats have high amounts of protein in their milk. C: Nubian goats and spiders both prefer living in large groups. D: Nubian goats and spiders have body parts that are similar.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q32.alt: Question 32. In the process described in lines 35 through 47, which step allows the threads to become strong enough for surgical procedures? Choices: A: The silk proteins are turned into clumps. B: The silk is stretched repeatedly. C: Salt is added to the goat’s milk. D: Water is added to thin the clumps.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q33.alt: Question 33. Why are lines 55 through 59 important to the article? Choices: A: They suggest that the goats are unusual. B: They explain how the goats are kept busy. C: They explain that the goats are treated well. D: They suggest that the goats are like humans.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q34.alt: Question 34. Which statement best expresses a central idea of the article? Choices: A: Nubian goats produce better quality silk than spiders. B: Spider silk is a complex substance that takes effort to make. C: Nubian goats have been genetically altered to produce spider silk. D: Spider silk contains proteins that are similar to proteins in other living things.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q35.alt: Question 35. Which detail is most important to include in a summary of the article? Choices: A: Scientists have made an attempt to gather silk from spiders living on farms. B: Spider silk has qualities that can be used in many products. C: A scientist started a company to research goat silk. D: Machines spin spider silk into thin threads.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q36.alt: Question 36. How does Nina’s attitude toward Ms. Charlemagne change? Choices: A: Nina becomes less interested after noticing Ms. Charlemagne’s bracelets. B: Nina becomes more fascinated after learning Ms. Charlemagne’s name. C: Nina becomes less surprised after hearing Ms. Charlemagne’s viewpoints. D: Nina becomes more suspicious after hearing Ms. Charlemagne’s assignment.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q37.alt: Question 37. How do lines 34 through 39 contribute to the development of the story? Choices: A: by suggesting that Ms. Charlemagne is not qualified to teach art B: by introducing Nina to a new way to think about art C: by showing that Ms. Charlemagne does not understand how artists work D: by describing the way Nina usually completes art assignments

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q38.alt: Question 38. Why does the author use the word “chirped” in line 43 of the story? Choices: A: to reveal that Ms. Charlemagne has creative ideas B: to imply that Ms. Charlemagne is new at teaching art C: to demonstrate that Ms. Charlemagne has a cheerful outlook D: to show that Ms. Charlemagne easily relates to the art students

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q39.alt: Question 39. Read this sentence from line 54. I could change that number, at least here if not in real life. How does this sentence best contribute to the development of the story? Choices: A: by signaling a change in Nina’s thinking B: by emphasizing the importance of the setting C: by revealing Nina’s strong feelings D: by suggesting a new plot development

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q40.alt: Question 40. Which quotation best supports a theme of the story? Choices: A: “Still, I knew she liked the picture and felt proud to see it up on the wall.” (lines 8 and 9) B: “I had never seen anything like them.” (line 18) C: “Imagine being descended from a medieval French king!” (lines 30 and 31) D: “I was the lord of my own picture, wasn’t I?” (line 56)

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q41.alt: Question 41. Based on details in the story, what can readers conclude about Ms. Charlemagne? Choices: A: She is a respected artist. B: She has a famous relative. C: She has a unique personality. D: She is a popular substitute teacher.

nysed-ela-2017-g6-mc-q42.alt: Question 42. How do the details in the story help develop a theme? Choices: A: Nina’s thoughts about her mother help develop the theme that being honest will make you feel better. B: Nina’s interaction with Isobel helps develop the theme that experiencing a new situation is easier with a friend. C: Nina’s drawing helps develop the theme that expressing yourself can help you work through your struggles. D: Nina’s description of Ms. Charlemagne helps develop the theme that judging others by their appearance is not a good idea.
